title: Dynamic Programming: Memoization vs Tabulation
description: Formally naming Dynamic Programming, revisiting the Fibonacci memoization from Recursion in DSA, and introducing tabulation -- building answers bottom-up instead of top-down.
objectives: Understand overlapping sub-problems and optimal substructure -- the two properties DP needs
Recognize memoization (top-down) as what Recursion in DSA already demonstrated
Implement tabulation (bottom-up) for the same problem, and compare the two approaches

sections.text: In Recursion in DSA, naive Fibonacci was O(2^n) due to repeated work, and we fixed it with a HashMap cache -- that fix already WAS Dynamic Programming (DP). Today we name it formally and add its second technique: tabulation.

A technique for solving problems by breaking them into overlapping sub-problems, solving each sub-problem only once, and storing (caching) its result for reuse -- applicable when a problem has both overlapping sub-problems and optimal substructure.

sections.title: The two conditions DP requires
rows: The same smaller sub-problem is needed multiple times (fib(3) needed by both fib(5) and fib(4))
An optimal solution to the whole problem can be built from optimal solutions to its sub-problems (same requirement Greedy Algorithms also needed)

sections.text: Solving a problem recursively, exactly as you naturally would, but caching each sub-problem's result the first time it's computed and reusing it on later requests.
Solving a problem iteratively by filling a table (usually an array) starting from the smallest base cases, building up to the final answer -- no recursion involved.

sections.title: Tracing fibTabulation(10)'s dp array as it fills
Memoization vs tabulation
rows: Starts from the ORIGINAL problem, recurses down to base cases
Starts from BASE cases, builds up to the original problem
Deep recursion can StackOverflowError on very large n (Recursion Basics)
No recursion -- no stack overflow risk
Only the ones actually needed for this call
Typically all of them, in order, even ones not strictly required

sections.text: Both approaches give the SAME correct answer and the SAME O(n) time complexity for Fibonacci -- the choice between them is about STYLE and practical constraints (like avoiding deep recursion), not correctness. Later DP lessons (1D DP, 2D DP) will use both styles depending on which fits the problem more naturally.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Assuming memoization and tabulation are entirely different algorithms -- they solve the same problem with the same underlying recurrence, just built in opposite directions.
Forgetting tabulation needs its base cases set explicitly BEFORE the loop starts filling in the rest of the table.
Using memoization on a problem with a very large n, risking a StackOverflowError that tabulation would have avoided entirely.

Trying to apply DP to a problem that lacks overlapping sub-problems -- if every sub-problem is genuinely distinct (like plain factorial), there's nothing to cache, and DP offers no benefit over simple recursion.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What two properties must a problem have for Dynamic Programming to apply?
questions.answer: Overlapping sub-problems (the same smaller sub-problem is needed multiple times) and optimal substructure (an optimal solution to the whole problem can be built from optimal solutions to its sub-problems).

questions.question: What is the difference between memoization and tabulation?
questions.answer: Memoization is top-down: solve the problem recursively as you naturally would, caching each sub-problem's result the first time it's computed. Tabulation is bottom-up: iteratively fill a table starting from the base cases, building up to the final answer, with no recursion.

questions.question: Why might tabulation be preferred over memoization for very large inputs?
questions.answer: Memoization relies on recursion, which can exhaust the call stack and cause a StackOverflowError for very large inputs. Tabulation is purely iterative, avoiding that risk entirely.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Dynamic Programming applies to problems with overlapping sub-problems and optimal substructure, solving each distinct sub-problem only once instead of recomputing it. Memoization (top-down) solves the problem recursively as naturally written, caching each sub-problem's result on first computation -- exactly what fixed naive Fibonacci's O(2^n) blowup in Recursion in DSA. Tabulation (bottom-up) instead iteratively fills a table starting from the base cases up to the final answer, with no recursion and therefore no stack overflow risk. Both give the same correct answer and complexity for a given problem; the choice is about style and practical constraints, not correctness.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: Memoization = solve top-down, jot the answer in the margin as you go (recursive + cache). Tabulation = solve bottom-up, filling a table row by row from the smallest case upward (iterative + table). Same destination, opposite starting points.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: What two properties does a problem need for DP to apply?
What's the key difference between memoization and tabulation?
Why might tabulation avoid a risk that memoization has for very large inputs?
Do memoization and tabulation give different answers for the same problem?
